source.title: Prioritized memory access explains planning and hippocampal replay

confidence_rationale: Normative theory (expected value of backup: gain x need) that predicts which memories should be prioritised at each moment. The theory directly predicts that staleness-proportional priority falls out of a well-specified utility function -- gain scales with the discrepancy between stored value and true value (staleness), need scales with likelihood of future access. This is the closest extant normative backbone for MECH-285. Confidence is moderated by 'theory not biology' -- the paper reproduces replay phenomenology but does not itself measure staleness or V_s residuals.
failure_signatures: A single normative objective (EVB = gain x need) unifies multiple previously-disparate replay phenomena (forward/reverse, rewarded-location bias, planning preplay) under one priority scheme.
EVB predicts that both proportional and situation-contingent priorities fall out of the same objective -- the shape of the priority signal is not assumed but derived from the utility function.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Replay content at any moment can be predicted by a normative rule: simulate the memory whose access most reduces expected future regret, given the current state. This decomposes into gain (how much a backup improves future choice quality) times need (how likely the memory is to be useful soon).
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-285 posits that sleep-replay priority is biased by accumulated V_s staleness. Mattar & Daw give the theoretical grounding: staleness is structurally equivalent to the 'gain' term -- regions where the stored model is most wrong are regions where an update most improves future choice. Under a well-specified EVB, staleness-proportional priority is not an arbitrary architectural choice but the normatively correct one. This also predicts that the proportional-vs-threshold question resolves in favour of proportional: EVB is continuous in both gain and need, so a threshold-gated scheme would be biologically suboptimal.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This is a theory paper, not a direct measurement. The EVB framework matches replay phenomenology well but is not itself evidence for any specific biological implementation. MECH-285's dissociation claim (staleness separable from salience) is not addressed directly -- in the EVB framework, salience (reward-related need) and staleness (gain) both feed the same priority signal compositionally, which is compatible with either dissociable or composed implementations.
mapping.source_context: Computational model; simulations of canonical spatial navigation tasks; normative derivation validated against multiple published hippocampal replay datasets.

confidence_components.notes: Theory-first paper with strong empirical coverage. Mapping fidelity high because the normative prediction (staleness-proportional priority) almost directly underwrites MECH-285. Main risk is over-relying on a model to settle an empirical question.
